The front door gapes wide, and through it they can see their mother sitting on the bottom stair, her body curled inward like a question mark, her hands over her face. There is no sign of their father in the yard, no looming figure at the gate.

They approach like penitents, bare feet silent on the concrete, and Ethan steps through the doorway first. The house smells wrong—violence and lavender and the sharp ozone scent of adrenaline. The wedding photo from the bedroom lies shattered on the entry tile, glass sprayed across the floor like diamonds.

Their mother looks up, her mascara tracking black tributaries down her cheeks, and her expression fractures between relief and a hatred so pure it makes Maya step back, her heel crunching on the glass.

"Where," her mother whispers, her voice destroyed, "did you go?"

"Nowhere," Maya says, the lie automatic, her throat raw. "We were scared. Dad was... he was scaring us."

"Scaring you?" The voice booms from the kitchen, and their father emerges, his tie loosened, his hair wild, holding the stained bedsheets from the closet in one fist like a battle standard. He looks at them with eyes that have gone beyond anger into some terrible, calm place. "You run from me. You hide. You destroy my home, and you say I scare you?"

He throws the sheets at their feet. The damp fabric unfurls across the tile, the evidence of their afternoon landing with a wet slap that seems to echo through the house.

"Explain this," he commands, pointing at the dark stains, the smell of it rising now, undeniable, biological. "Explain why my daughter’s bed smells like a brothel. Explain why my son looks at his sister like..." He cannot finish the sentence, his hand going to his chest, clutching at his shirt over his heart.

Maya drops to her knees, not in prayer but in performance, her hands finding the damp cotton, pressing her face into it, inhaling the scent of their crime even as she sobs. "It’s just mine, Dad, I was touching myself, I was exploring, I’m a sinner, I’m dirty, Ethan walked in and I was embarrassed and that’s why we fought, that’s why he ran, because he saw me naked and I was ashamed—"

The lie is baroque, absurd, but it offers him an out, a lesser evil to cling to, and she sees him seize it, his shoulders dropping, his face cycling through disgust and desperate relief. Masturbation. Self-abuse. Not the other thing. Not the unthinkable thing.

"Lord help me," he mutters, turning away, his hand over his mouth.

Their mother stands up, unsteady, and crosses to Ethan, gripping his chin with fingers that dig into his jaw, forcing his face up to the light. "Look at me," she hisses. "Tell me she’s telling the truth. Tell me you didn’t touch her."

Ethan’s eyes are dead, flat, perfectly controlled. "I didn’t touch her," he says, the words precise and hollow. "I saw her. I shouldn’t have. I’m sorry."

She releases him like he’s burning her, stumbling back against the banister.

"Upstairs," their father says, his back still turned, his voice muffled. "Both of you. Separate rooms. You do not come out until I say. You do not speak to each other. You do not use phones, computers, anything. We are going to pray, and then we are going to decide what to do with you."

Maya stands, leaving the sheet on the floor, and walks toward the stairs, her head down, the picture of shame. As she passes Ethan, their shoulders brush, and in that microsecond of contact, she presses a folded scrap of paper into his hand—a leaf from the hedge, rough and veined, a reminder of what waits beneath the dirt.

They ascend in single file, Maya first, then Ethan, their mother watching them with eyes that see everything and nothing, their father kneeling now in the foyer, his forehead pressed against the wall, whispering psalms to the drywall.

In her room, Maya finds the drawers turned out, her underwear scattered, but the phone is gone, buried safely outside. She sits on the stripped mattress, her bleeding feet leaving rusty smears on the fitted sheet, and listens to the sound of her father’s weeping rising from below, a sound like tearing fabric.

She counts the hours until Tuesday.

The mattress springs dig into Maya’s thighs, bare and sticky against the rubberized ticking where the sheets have been stripped away. She sits with her spine pressed to the wall, knees drawn up beneath the skirt of her sundress, listening to the cadence of her father’s prayers below shift from the broken, gasping rhythm of shock into the mechanical recitation of Psalm 51. Create in me a clean heart, O God. The words seep through the floorboards, each syllable enunciated with the precision of a man who believes that if he speaks the scripture loudly enough, it will scrub the sin from his drywall, from his children’s skin, from the air itself.

Hours pass, measured not by the clock—confiscated, along with her laptop, her tablet, the old iPod touch she’d forgotten in a drawer—but by the shifting rectangles of light that move across her ceiling as the sun abandons the sky. Her mother brings her food at what must be dinner, a paper plate with a sandwich that tastes like refrigerator plastic and guilt, placed on the floor without a word, the door cracked only wide enough for a hand and a plate to enter, as if Maya’s corruption might be airborne.

"Mom," Maya says, her voice cracking from disuse, from screaming on the roof.

The hand pauses, tendons standing out like guy wires beneath papery skin.

"Can I have my Bible?"

It is the right thing to say. The hand withdraws, returns moments later with the leather-bound book, the one with her name embossed in gold on the lower right corner, a gift from her confirmation two years prior. Her mother pushes it through the gap, and for a moment their fingers brush, and Maya feels the tremor running through her mother’s body, a vibration of betrayal and love so tangled it feels like electricity.

"Thank you," Maya whispers.

The door clicks shut. The lock turns—a new addition, she realizes, installed in the hours she’s been imprisoned, the brass fixture gleaming with fresh screw marks in the jamb.

She opens the Bible not to read, but to hide. Between the pages of Lamentations, she finds the leaf Ethan passed her, still green, still alive with chlorophyll and hope. She tucks it into her bra, against her heart, where the phone had been.

Down the hall, she hears Ethan’s door open, the shuffle of her father’s heavy footsteps, the murmur of masculine voices that sound like interrogation. Her father’s voice drops into the register he uses for elder board meetings, for serious discussions about church finances, for casting out demons. Ethan’s responses are muffled, monosyllabic, the sound of a boy retreating deep inside himself where they cannot follow.

Later, when the house has gone silent except for the refrigerator’s hum and the crickets outside, Maya hears the scratching. At first she thinks it’s mice in the walls, the old house settling, but it comes again—tap, tap-scratch—rhythmic, deliberate. She crawls to the wall shared with Ethan’s room, pressing her ear against the plaster, cool and chalky against her cheek.

Tap. Tap-tap. Tap.

Morse code. They learned it in Boy Scouts, before Ethan quit because the troop leader said video games were tools of Satan. She deciphers it slowly, her lips moving to shape the letters.

B. U. R. I. E. D. S. A. F. E.

She taps back, her fingernail against the drywall, hoping the sound carries through the insulation. T. U. E. S. D. A. Y.

A pause. Then, frantic, rapid: M. O. M. S. U. S. P. I. C. I. O. U. S.

Maya closes her eyes. She can picture her mother downstairs, not sleeping, sitting at the kitchen table with the stained sheets in a Ziploc bag, examining them like forensic evidence, her faith warring with her intuition. Religious mothers have a sense for these things, a radar for the corruption of innocence that operates independently of logic or proof. Maya knows her mother is replaying every glance between her and Ethan, every time they were alone in the house, every "yoga session" and "movie night," assembling the architecture of their sin brick by brick.

Morning comes with the sound of her father’s boots on the stairs, heavy and deliberate. The lock turns. He stands in the doorway, his face gray with sleeplessness, holding two garments draped over his arm—her modesty swimsuit, the one-piece with the skirt attached that she hasn’t worn since she was twelve, and Ethan’s baptismal robe, white and scratchy, pulled from the cedar chest.

"Church," he says, his voice a gravel road. "Pastor wants to see you both. Before the service. Before the congregation arrives."

Maya stands, her legs numb, the leaf crinkling against her breast. "Dad, please, we’re not dressed, we haven’t eaten—"

"Fasting," he interrupts, his eyes not meeting hers, fixed on a point above her head as if she has become something unclean, a vessel of filth that might contaminate his gaze. "Prayer. Confession. This is what we’re doing now. This is how we fix what you’ve broken."

He steps back, allowing her to exit, and she walks down the hallway with her head bowed, passing Ethan’s room where he stands in the doorway wearing only his boxers and the baptismal robe thrown over his shoulders like a shawl, his face blank, his eyes finding hers for one blazing second before dropping to the floor.

In the car, they sit in the backseat like prisoners being transported, the child locks engaged, their father driving with both hands on the wheel at ten and two, their mother staring out the passenger window, her rosary wrapped around her knuckles so tight the beads leave indentations in her flesh. The radio is off. The only sound is the whisper of the tires on asphalt and the occasional wet sniff from her mother’s direction.

Maya watches the hedges pass, the one where the phone waits like a ticking bomb, buried in mulch and darkness. She counts the blocks until Tuesday, until freedom, until the Marriott where Vivian and Julian wait with their cameras and their kindness and their terrible, patient hunger.

Ethan admitted to curiosity, to pornography. These are failings, yes, but they are failings of the flesh that every saint has battled. They are not... what you feared."

He looks directly at their father, holding his gaze with the authority of a man who has buried their congregation’s dead and christened their infants. "You accused your children of abomination based on a digital lie. You terrorized them. Look at them."

Their father turns. Maya meets his eyes, letting her bottom lip tremble, letting the fear show—the fear of the roof, the chase, the buried phone—recasting it as the trauma of false accusation. His face crumples, the purple rage draining into ashen horror at his own behavior.

The request for the purity ring comes on Monday morning, delivered with downcast eyes and fingers twisting in the hem of her modesty skirt—the denim one that falls past her knees, retrieved from the back of the closet where it had been exiled since she turned fourteen. Maya waits until her father is drinking his coffee, the black liquid trembling in the ceramic mug with the fish symbol on it, his hands still uncertain after yesterday’s violence.

"I want to consecrate myself," she says, her voice barely audible above the refrigerator’s hum, her posture arranged to suggest both fragility and resolve. "After what happened... what people thought... I want something visible. A covenant."

Her mother’s eyes fill immediately, the tears of a woman desperate to believe in redemption. Her father sets down the mug with a click that sounds like a period at the end of a sentence.

"Your mother has her grandmother’s ring," he says, his voice thick. "The sapphire. It was meant for your wedding day, but... yes. A promise to yourself. To God."

Maya extends her left hand, palm up, and feels the cool weight of the antique gold sliding onto her finger, the stone dark blue as a bruise. She closes her fist around it and smiles, a tremulous expression that masks the calculation beneath—this ring is currency, a visible token of her rehabilitation that will buy her hours of unsupervised time, solitary walks for "prayer and reflection," bathroom locks that will not be questioned.

On Monday night, while her parents attend an emergency elder board meeting to discuss the "deepfake scandal" affecting the youth group, Maya slips out the back door. The moon is a thin fingernail scraping the sky, providing just enough light to navigate the hedge. She digs with her bare hands, nails raking through mulch and loam, until her fingers brush the hard rectangle of the case.

The phone is dead, the battery drained after three days in the dirt. She smuggles it inside wrapped in her Bible cover, charging it beneath her pillow while she feigns sleep, the heat of the charging port burning against her cheek like a coal.

When the screen blinks to life at 2:00 AM, the notification icon explodes—2441 unread messages, 18,000 new followers, a banner announcing "TRENDING: #3 ON CARNALCRYPT" in pulsing red letters. The site’s name glows against the black background, the word "Carnal" in serif font like a university press, "Crypt" in something jagged and Gothic beneath it, a digital mausoleum where the living post their deaths.

Maya scrolls with her thumb, the light painting her face in shifting blues. Their video—the kitchen counter scene, the wedding photo visible in the background—has 1,340,000 views. The comments scroll in a manic river:

"Real siblings confirmed, look at the nose bridge genetics"
"she’s so tight, can’t be older than 16"
"post the anal training already"
"Scholarly_Pursuits is right, this is art"

Her crypto wallet balance glows at $14,892. Nearly fifteen thousand dollars, triple what Vivian and Julian promised for Tuesday alone. The money hums in digital stasis, waiting to be converted to cash, to bus tickets, to freedom.

She opens the private messages. Scholarly_Pursuits has sent seven new texts, the timestamps marching across Sunday while she was singing hymns:

Saturday, 11:47 PM: "We’ve seen the news about the deepfake scandal in your town. Clever children. We understand camouflage."

Sunday, 8:15 AM: "The Marriott reservation is confirmed. Suite 412. We’ll arrive Monday evening to prepare the space. Medical grade enemas, three sizes of dilators, sterile lubricant. Julian is bringing his Arri Alexa."

Sunday, 9:00 PM: "We’ve deposited an additional advance. Check your wallet. Consider it compensation for your distress."

Maya’s breath quickens, her heart hammering against her ribs. She opens the wallet. Another five thousand has appeared, the transaction hash tracing back to an account labeled "Cultivated Holdings LLC."

A new message arrives as she watches, the timestamp 2:04 AM, current:

"We’re in town. Watching the house now. Your bedroom light is on. You look beautiful in your repentance."

Maya freezes. Her eyes snap to the window, the blinds drawn but trembling slightly from the air conditioning vent. Outside, the street is empty, the Hendersons’ house dark, the oak tree a skeletal silhouette. But somewhere in that darkness, Vivian and Julian sit in a parked car with tinted windows, watching her glow, watching her sin, waiting like patient spiders for Tuesday’s web to tighten.

She types back with shaking thumbs: "How do you know which window is mine?"

The response is immediate: "The one with the Bible verse decal. Leviticus 18. We appreciate the irony."

Maya’s blood turns to ice. The decal, peeling at the edges, a gift from her mother three years ago, marking her room as the dwelling place of purity while inside she plans her defilement.

Footsteps creak in the hallway—Ethan, unable to sleep, pacing. She opens her door a crack, hissing his name, and he slips inside, his face gaunt in the phone’s blue light. She shows him the screen, the balance, the messages, the admission that they are being watched.

"They’re here," she whispers, her lips barely moving.

Ethan looks at the window, his Adam’s apple bobbing. "Do we call it off?"

Maya closes the phone, the darkness rushing back in, and takes his hand, pressing it against her chest where the purity ring sits heavy and false. "We take their money," she breathes. "All of it. We let them teach us. And when they think they own us, we disappear with what we’ve learned."

She guides his hand lower, beneath the waistband of her pajama shorts, showing him that she is already wet, already ready, her body betraying the ring on her finger with a slick heat that has nothing to do with salvation. Ethan’s breath hitches, his fingers sliding through the sparse hair, finding the swollen flesh that throbs for the camera, for the money, for the strangers in the dark who think they are hunting lambs but have actually cornered something sharper.

"Tomorrow," she promises, arching against his palm, the phone buzzing once more with a new notification from CarnalCrypt: "User Scholarly_Pursuits has sent a photo attachment."

She doesn’t open it yet. She lets the anticipation build, lets Ethan stroke her until her hips lift off the mattress, until the ring on her hand catches the moonlight and throws it against the ceiling like a challenge to God. Outside, an engine turns over, purrs once, and fades into the night—the watchers retreating, satisfied for now, leaving the siblings to their rehearsal for the main event.
